summary: Drew DeVault recounts his three-year experiment using mobile Linux (postmarketOS and UBports) as his daily driver across multiple devices, ultimately switching back to LineageOS (Android). Despite being impressed by the progress and capabilities of mobile Linux, particularly postmarketOS running Alpine Linux, he found the reliability of core telephony features—calls and SMS—unacceptably inconsistent. He argues that mobile Linux cannot be a viable daily driver until the community prioritizes rock-solid phone functionality above all else.
key_points: After three years of daily-driving mobile Linux across four devices, DeVault switched back to LineageOS due to reliability issues
Core telephony (calls and SMS) is the non-negotiable baseline for a phone OS, and mobile Linux consistently failed to deliver it
postmarketOS offered the most compelling feature set but suffered from constant regressions where each update broke different things
UBports had better initial telephony reliability but lacked useful auxiliary features and eventually developed SMS issues too
The mobile Linux community needs to focus singularly on always being usable as a phone before expanding features
money_quotes: What put the final nails in the coffin was what I have been calling out as my main concern throughout my experience: reliability, particularly of the telephony components.
My phone is a critical communication device and I need to be able to depend on calls and SMS at all times.
I remember struggling with postmarketOS while I was sick with a terrible throat infection – and I could not call my doctor. Not cool.
Being able to run the same environment I run everywhere else, Alpine Linux, on my phone, is fucking amazing.
On edge and on stable it seemed like every update broke some things and fixed others, so only a subset of these cool features was working well at any given moment.
Before anyone can be expected to use this as a daily driver, the community really needs to batten down the hatches and focus on one thing and one thing only: always, always being usable as a phone.
key_insight: Mobile Linux has made impressive progress on features, but until the community prioritizes unbreakable core telephony over everything else, it cannot serve as a daily driver.
